items.myth: MLflow and W&B replace the need for train/val/test discipline.
items.reality: Tracking tools record what your script computes — they do not fix leakage or wrong splits. You must still evaluate on the correct held-out set and log that metric consistently.
items.example: Logging `val_f1` computed on the training set still yields a useless comparison table with pretty charts.

items.myth: log_param and log_metric are interchangeable in MLflow.
items.reality: Params are for static config (one value per key, stringified); metrics support numeric time series with optional `step`. Using metrics for hyperparameters loses type clarity and UI semantics.

items.myth: You must pick either MLflow or W&B — never both.
items.reality: They solve overlapping but not identical problems. Teams often use W&B for experiment visibility and MLflow for local registry-centric deployment, or standardize on one tool per project.
items.example: Prototype with W&B dashboards; register production candidates via MLflow `register_model` from the best run.

items.myth: Artifacts are optional if metrics look good.
items.reality: Without artifacts you cannot reload the exact weights, plots, or environment files from a run — metrics alone cannot redeploy or audit the model binary.
items.example: Run shows `val_auc=0.92` but no `model.pkl` artifact — promotion to staging is blocked until retrain.
